ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general edamame food product entry; no specific stir-fried edamame with sauce variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers immature soya beans (edamame) as a raw commodity. No stir-fried prepared variant with sauce exists in FoodEx2; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT has a substance-level entry for edamame; no prepared stir-fried dish code exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of stir-fried edamame with special sauce is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one shared side-dish plate as commonly served in Singaporean restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Edamame, frozen, prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168411); adapted for stir-fried preparation with sauce additions
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy entry for prepared edamame (FDC ID 168411). Values adjusted upward for sodium and fat to account for stir-frying with soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, and a savoury special sauce typical of Singaporean restaurant preparation. Energy, protein, and carbohydrate values are consistent with the USDA base with minor sauce-contribution adjustments. This is the best available approved-source anchor for this dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; soybeans / edamame GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried edamame with special sauce. GI of 18 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for soybeans / green soybeans (edamame), which consistently show GI values in the range 15–20 across multiple peer-reviewed studies (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The stir-frying process and sauce additions do not materially alter the GI of the legume base. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (18 × 12.75) / 100 ≈ 2.3 for a 150 g serving (8.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.5 = 12.75 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and special sauce
hypertension.guidance: Each 150 g serving contributes approximately 480 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation to keep daily sodium within the recommended 2,000 mg limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI 18) and high dietary fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried edamame has a very low GI of 18 and provides 4.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g, making it an excellent choice for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the glycemic load per serving is approximately 2.3, posing minimal postprandial glucose impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High plant protein, low saturated fat, and beneficial polyunsaturated fatty acids from soy
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Edamame is rich in plant-based protein and polyunsaturated fats (including omega-6 fatty acids) with low saturated fat (0.8 g per 100 g), supporting a heart-healthy dietary pattern; however, the sodium from the sauce should be monitored in individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Edamame with Special Sauce (毛豆炒特色酱) is a vegetarian side dish popular in Singaporean restaurants, featuring tender immature green soybeans (edamame) wok-fried at high heat with a savoury special sauce typically composed of soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, chilli, and a touch of sugar or oyster-style vegetarian sauce. The dish delivers a satisfying combination of textures — the edamame pods are lightly charred on the outside while remaining tender and slightly firm inside, with a rich umami-forward flavour profile balanced by mild heat and nuttiness from sesame. Nutritionally, edamame is one of the most protein-dense plant foods available, providing approximately 10 g protein per 100 g, along with 4.2 g dietary fibre, significant folate (303 mcg DFE), iron (2.1 mg), and potassium (436 mg). Its very low glycemic index of 18 makes it particularly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or seeking low-GI plant-based protein sources. The dish is naturally cholesterol-free and low in saturated fat, supporting cardiovascular health, though the sodium content from the sauce (approximately 320 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for those managing hypertension. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served as a shared starter or side dish alongside rice or noodle mains, and is widely available at Chinese-style zi char restaurants and Vietnamese-fusion eateries. The dish is labelled vegetarian and is suitable for lacto-ovo vegetarians; vegans should verify the sauce composition.
common_user_queries: How many calories in edamame with sauce?
What is the GI of edamame?
Is stir-fried edamame high in sodium?
Can I eat edamame if I have high blood pressure?
What is 毛豆炒特色酱?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for stir-fried edamame with special sauce as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA SR Legacy entry for prepared edamame (FDC ID 168411) with reasonable adjustments for sauce and stir-frying contributions. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for green soybeans/edamame, which is a well-established low-GI food. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base ingredient (edamame) is well-characterised in approved sources and the adaptations are conservative and documented.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' (input) to 'Vegetables' as edamame is the primary ingredient and the dish is a vegetable-legume preparation. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy source). 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing B12; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for edamame). 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — edamame and plant-based sauces contain no meaningful vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy). 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based dish; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for edamame). 6. Sodium value of 320 mg per 100 g is an upward adaptation from the USDA base (approximately 6 mg per 100 g for plain prepared edamame) to reflect the soy sauce and special sauce used in restaurant preparation; this estimate is conservative and consistent with typical stir-fried vegetable dishes with soy-based sauces. 7. Vegan status set to false pending confirmation of sauce composition (oyster sauce or fish sauce may be present despite vegetarian labelling). 8. Halal status set to false as the restaurant (Viet'Spice) is not confirmed halal-certified. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g values are sourced from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profile for edamame. 10. Sentinel value -1 is not used in this record; all fields are populated with numeric values from the approved source or set to 0.0 with justification as noted above.
